A **personal union** is a combination of two or more [monarchical](/wiki/Monarchy "Monarchy") [states](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)") that have the same [monarch](/wiki/Monarch "Monarch") while their boundaries, laws, and interests remain distinct.[[1]](#cite_note-oppenheim1-1) A [real union](/wiki/Real_union "Real union"), by contrast, involves the constituent states being to some extent interlinked, such as by sharing some limited governmental institutions. It differs from a [federation](/wiki/Federation "Federation") in that each constituent state has an independent government, whereas a federal state is united by a central government. The ruler in a personal union does not need to be a hereditary monarch.[[note 1]](#cite_note-2)

The term was coined by German jurist [Johann Stephan Pütter](/wiki/Johann_Stephan_P%C3%BCtter "Johann Stephan Pütter"), introducing it into *Elementa iuris publici germanici* (Elements of German Public Law) of 1760.[[2]](#cite_note-3)

Personal unions can arise for several reasons, such as:

* inheritance through a [dynastic union](/wiki/Dynastic_union "Dynastic union"), e.g., [Louis X of France](/wiki/Louis_X_of_France "Louis X of France") inherited [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") from [his father](/wiki/Philip_IV_of_France "Philip IV of France") and [Navarre](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Navarre "Kingdom of Navarre") from [his mother](/wiki/Joan_I_of_Navarre "Joan I of Navarre")
* [decolonization](/wiki/Decolonization "Decolonization"), where ex-colonies install the monarch of the former colonizing power as their own upon becoming independent, e.g., several former members of the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") (then becoming [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm"))
* [autonomization](/wiki/Autonomy "Autonomy"), e.g., instead of annexing [Finland](/wiki/Finland_under_Swedish_rule "Finland under Swedish rule") into the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), [Alexander I of Russia](/wiki/Alexander_I_of_Russia "Alexander I of Russia") organized Finland as [an autonomous grand duchy](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Finland "Grand Duchy of Finland") and acted as its [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state")[[3]](#cite_note-4)

They can also be [codified](/wiki/Codification_(law) "Codification (law)") (i.e., the constitutions of the states clearly express that they shall share the same person as head of state) or non-codified, in which case they can easily be broken (e.g., by the death of the monarch when the two states have different [succession laws](/wiki/Order_of_succession "Order of succession")).

The concept of a personal union has only very rarely crossed over from monarchies into [republics](/wiki/Republic "Republic").

There are currently two personal unions in the world: the 15 [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm"), who share [Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") as their head of state, and one of the [co-princes of Andorra](/wiki/Co-princes_of_Andorra "Co-princes of Andorra") being the [President of France](/wiki/President_of_France "President of France").

#### Congo Free State and Belgium

* [Kingdom of Kartli](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kartli "Kingdom of Kartli") and [Kingdom of Kakheti](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kakheti "Kingdom of Kakheti") united under the rule of a single monarch in 1513–1520 ([David X](/wiki/David_X_of_Kartli "David X of Kartli")), 1625–1633 ([Teimuraz I](/wiki/Teimuraz_I_of_Kakheti "Teimuraz I of Kakheti")), 1648–1658 ([Rostom](/wiki/Rostom_of_Kartli "Rostom of Kartli")), 1660–1664 ([Vakhtang V](/wiki/Vakhtang_V_of_Kartli "Vakhtang V of Kartli")), 1723 ([Constantine II/III](/wiki/Constantine_II_of_Kakheti "Constantine II of Kakheti")), to finally unite the [Kingdom of Kartli-Kakheti](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Kartli-Kakheti "Kingdom of Kartli-Kakheti") in 1762 under the reign of [Heraclius II](/wiki/Heraclius_II_of_Georgia "Heraclius II of Georgia") and his descendants.
* [Kingdom of Imereti](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Imereti "Kingdom of Imereti") and [Principality of Guria](/wiki/Principality_of_Guria "Principality of Guria") united under the rule of a single monarch in 1681–1683 ([George IV/III](/wiki/George_III_of_Guria "George III of Guria")), 1701–1702, 1713–1714 ([Mamia (III)](/wiki/Mamia_III_Gurieli "Mamia III Gurieli")) and 1720 ([George VIII/IV](/wiki/George_IV_of_Guria "George IV of Guria")).

#### Goryeo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_union&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Goryeo")]

* Personal union with [Shenyang](/wiki/Shenyang "Shenyang") in the [Mongol Empire](/wiki/Mongol_Empire "Mongol Empire") (1308–1313; 1345-1351)
  + As King of Goryeo (高麗國王) and Prince/King of Shenyang (瀋陽王) in 1308–1310
  + As King of Goryeo and Prince/King of Shen (瀋王) in 1310–1313, 1345-1351

King [Chungseon of Goryeo](/wiki/Chungseon_of_Goryeo "Chungseon of Goryeo") reigned as King of Goryeo in 1298 and 1308–1313 and as King of Shenyang or King of Shen from 1307 (according to the *[History of Yuan](/wiki/History_of_Yuan "History of Yuan")*) or 1308 (according to *[Goryeosa](/wiki/Goryeosa "Goryeosa")*) to 1316. At that time, Goryeo had already become a vassal of the Mongol Empire and the Mongol imperial family following the [Mongol invasions of Korea](/wiki/Mongol_invasions_of_Korea "Mongol invasions of Korea") and the Goryeo royal family had established strong ties by intermarrying with the Mongol imperial family. Because King Chungseon was a very powerful man during the reign of [Külüg Khan](/wiki/K%C3%BCl%C3%BCg_Khan "Külüg Khan"), Külüg Khan gave him a new title on top of his kingship of Goryeo, the *Prince/King of [Shenyang](/wiki/Shenyang "Shenyang")*,[[note 2]](#cite_note-5)[[note 3]](#cite_note-6) in 1307[[4]](#cite_note-hoy22-7) or 1308 specifically mentioned as thanks to his efforts of bringing the Khan to power.[[5]](#cite_note-hog-8)

However, he lost his power in the Mongol imperial court after the death of the Külüg Khan. Because the [Mongol Empire](/wiki/Mongol_Empire "Mongol Empire") made [Chungseon](/wiki/Chungseon_of_Goryeo "Chungseon of Goryeo") abdicate the crown of the Goryeo in 1313, the personal union was ended. [King Chungsuk](/wiki/Chungsuk_of_Goryeo "Chungsuk of Goryeo"), Chungseon's eldest son, became the new King of Goryeo. In 1316, the Mongol Empire made Chungseon abdicate the crown of Shen in favour of [Wang Ko](/wiki/Wang_Ko "Wang Ko"), one of his nephews, resulting in him becoming the new King of Shen.

Following Wang Ko's death, [Chungmok of Goryeo](/wiki/Chungmok_of_Goryeo "Chungmok of Goryeo") and [Chungjeong of Goryeo](/wiki/Chungjeong_of_Goryeo "Chungjeong of Goryeo") were also dual rulers of both Goryeo and Shenyang from 1345-1351.

#### Andorra

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_union&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Andorra")]

Due to Andorra's special government form resulting from the [Paréage of 1278](/wiki/Par%C3%A9age_of_Andorra_(1278) "Paréage of Andorra (1278)"), it is a [diarchy](/wiki/Diarchy "Diarchy") with [co-princes](/wiki/Co-Princes_of_Andorra "Co-Princes of Andorra"). One of them is the [Bishop of Urgell](/wiki/Bishop_of_Urgell "Bishop of Urgell"); the other was originally the [Count of Foix](/wiki/Count_of_Foix "Count of Foix"). It is through this feudal co-prince system that the Principality has entered partial personal union with:

* [Kingdom of Navarre](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Navarre "Kingdom of Navarre") (1479–1620, then integrated into France).
* [Kingdom of France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") (1589–1792, [1814–1815](/wiki/First_Restoration "First Restoration"), [1815–1830](/wiki/Bourbon_Restoration_in_France "Bourbon Restoration in France"), [1830–1848](/wiki/July_Monarchy "July Monarchy")).

In 1607, the feudal co-prince was [Henry IV of France](/wiki/Henry_IV_of_France "Henry IV of France"), who issued an edict that his position should be held by the French Head of State. While the [new government](/wiki/French_First_Republic "French First Republic") did not take up the title during the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), all polities of France since 1806 regardless of their government form have accepted that their head of state is an ex officio co-prince. This led to personal unions with:

* [First](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire") and [Second](/wiki/Second_French_Empire "Second French Empire") French Empires (1806–1814, [1815](/wiki/Hundred_Days "Hundred Days") and 1852–1870).
* [Second](/wiki/French_Second_Republic "French Second Republic"), [Third](/wiki/French_Third_Republic "French Third Republic"), [Fourth](/wiki/French_Fourth_Republic "French Fourth Republic") and [Fifth](/wiki/French_Fifth_Republic "French Fifth Republic") [Republics of France](/wiki/France "France") (1848–1852, 1870–1940, 1944–1958, since 1958).
* [French State](/wiki/Vichy_France "Vichy France") (known as [Vichy France](/wiki/Government_of_Vichy_France "Government of Vichy France"), 1940–1944, in dispute with [Free France](/wiki/Free_France "Free France")).

* Personal union with [Croatia](/wiki/Croatia "Croatia") 1102–1918 (see [§ Croatia](#Croatia) above for details).
* Personal union with Poland and [Bohemia](/wiki/Lands_of_the_Bohemian_Crown "Lands of the Bohemian Crown") 1305.
* Personal union with Poland from 1370 to 1382 under the reign of [Louis the Great](/wiki/Louis_I_of_Hungary "Louis I of Hungary"). This period in Polish history is sometimes known as the *[Andegawen Poland](/wiki/Andegawen_Poland "Andegawen Poland")*. Louis inherited the Polish throne from his maternal uncle [Casimir III](/wiki/Casimir_III_of_Poland "Casimir III of Poland"). After Louis' death the Polish nobles (the *[szlachta](/wiki/Szlachta "Szlachta")*) decided to end the personal union, since they did not want to be governed from Hungary, and chose Louis' younger daughter [Jadwiga](/wiki/Jadwiga_of_Poland "Jadwiga of Poland") as their new ruler, while Hungary was inherited by his elder daughter [Mary](/wiki/Mary_of_Hungary "Mary of Hungary"). Personal union with Poland for the second time from 1440 to 1444.
* Personal union with Naples from 1385 to 1386 under the reign of [Charles III of Naples](/wiki/Charles_III_of_Naples "Charles III of Naples").
* Personal union with Bohemia, 1419–1439 (with both in interregnum during 1437–1438), 1453–1457 and 1490–1918.
* Personal union with the Archduchy of Austria, 1437–1439, 1444–1457, and 1526–1806.
* Personal union with the Holy Roman Empire, 1410–1439, 1556–1608, 1612–1740 and 1780–1806.
* Real union with Austria, 1867–1918 (the *[dual monarchy](/wiki/Dual_monarchy "Dual monarchy")* of [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary")) under the reigns of [Franz Joseph](/wiki/Franz_Joseph_of_Austria "Franz Joseph of Austria") and [Charles IV](/wiki/Karl_I_of_Austria "Karl I of Austria").

#### Saxe-Coburg and Saxe-Gotha

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_union&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Saxe-Coburg and Saxe-Gotha")]

In 1826, the newly created [Duchy of Saxe-Coburg and Gotha](/wiki/Duchy_of_Saxe-Coburg_and_Gotha "Duchy of Saxe-Coburg and Gotha") was initially a double duchy, ruled by Duke Ernest I in a personal union. In 1852, the duchies were bound in a political and real union. They were then a quasi-federal unitary state, even though later attempts to merge the duchies failed.

#### Saxe-Weimar and Saxe-Eisenach

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_union&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Saxe-Weimar and Saxe-Eisenach")]

The duchies of [Saxe-Weimar](/wiki/Saxe-Weimar "Saxe-Weimar") and [Saxe-Eisenach](/wiki/Saxe-Eisenach "Saxe-Eisenach") were in personal union from 1741, when the ruling house of Saxe-Eisenach died out, until 1809, when they were merged into the single duchy of [Saxe-Weimar-Eisenach](/wiki/Saxe-Weimar-Eisenach "Saxe-Weimar-Eisenach").

#### Schleswig and Holstein

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_union&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Schleswig and Holstein")]

*Duchies with peculiar rules for [succession](/wiki/Order_of_succession "Order of succession"). See the [Schleswig-Holstein Question](/wiki/Schleswig-Holstein_Question "Schleswig-Holstein Question").*

The kings of Denmark at the same time being dukes of Schleswig and Holstein 1460–1864. (Holstein being part of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"), while Schleswig was a part of Denmark). The situation was complicated by the fact that for some time, the Duchies were divided among collateral branches of the House of Oldenburg (the ruling House in Denmark and Schleswig-Holstein). Besides the "main" Duchy of Schleswig-Holstein-Glückstadt, ruled by the Kings of Denmark, there were states encompassing territory in both Duchies. Notably the Dukes of Schleswig-Holstein-Gottorp and the subordinate Dukes of Schleswig-Holstein-Beck, Schleswig-Holstein-Sonderburg-Augustenburg and Schleswig-Holstein-Sonderburg-Glücksburg.

#### Schwarzburg-Rudolstadt and Schwarzburg-Sondershausen

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_union&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Schwarzburg-Rudolstadt and Schwarzburg-Sondershausen")]

The duchies of [Schwarzburg-Rudolstadt](/wiki/Schwarzburg-Rudolstadt "Schwarzburg-Rudolstadt") and [Schwarzburg-Sondershausen](/wiki/Schwarzburg-Sondershausen "Schwarzburg-Sondershausen") were in personal union from 1909, when [Prince Günther](/wiki/G%C3%BCnther,_Prince_of_Schwarzburg "Günther, Prince of Schwarzburg") of Schwarzburg-Rudolstadt succeeded also to the throne of Schwarzburg-Sondershausen, until 1918, when he (and all the other German monarchs) abdicated.

* [Personal union](/wiki/Union_between_Sweden_and_Norway "Union between Sweden and Norway") with Norway (1814-1905).

#### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_union&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

* Personal union, with the [Principality of Orange](/wiki/Principality_of_Orange "Principality of Orange") and the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") (1689–1702) during the reign of [William of Orange](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England")
* Personal union with the [Electorate of Hanover](/wiki/Electorate_of_Hanover "Electorate of Hanover") (1801–1806).
* Personal union with the [Kingdom of Hanover](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hanover "Kingdom of Hanover") (1814–1837).

Historically, [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") of the United Kingdom was considered to be indivisible.[[6]](#cite_note-9) However, as the self-governing [dominions](/wiki/Dominion "Dominion") of the British Commonwealth gained control over the exercise of the royal prerogative in the 1930s, this concept has evolved so that 'the Crown [in right of](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/in_right_of "wikt:in right of")' each realm and territory acts independently of the other realms and territories.[[7]](#cite_note-Cox_2003-10) The [constitutional conventions](/wiki/Convention_(political_norm) "Convention (political norm)") established in the [Statute of Westminster 1931](/wiki/Statute_of_Westminster_1931 "Statute of Westminster 1931") which required uniformity in the laws of succession, along with a common format for the royal styles and titles, distinguished the Crown of the [Commonwealth realms](/wiki/Commonwealth_realm "Commonwealth realm") from a personal union, under which there is no alignment between multiple thrones and different laws of succession may exist but subsequent developments have made the situation more ambiguous.[[7]](#cite_note-Cox_2003-10)

### South America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_union&action=edit&section=50 "Edit section: South America")]

#### Brazil

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_union&action=edit&section=51 "Edit section: Brazil")]

* Personal union with Portugal, under [Pedro I of Brazil](/wiki/Pedro_I_of_Brazil "Pedro I of Brazil") (Pedro IV of Portugal), from 10 March to 28 May 1826. Pedro was the [Prince Royal of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves](/wiki/Prince_Royal_of_Portugal "Prince Royal of Portugal") when he declared the independence of Brazil in 1822, becoming its first emperor. When his father ([John VI of Portugal](/wiki/John_VI_of_Portugal "John VI of Portugal")) died, Pedro also became King of Portugal, but abdicated the Portuguese throne 79 days later in favour of his older child [Princess Maria da Glória](/wiki/Mary_II_of_Portugal "Mary II of Portugal").

#### Colombia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_union&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Colombia")]

* The [Free and Independent State of Cundinamarca](/wiki/Free_and_Independent_State_of_Cundinamarca "Free and Independent State of Cundinamarca") (modern-day [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia")) (1810–1813) was in a *[de iure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* personal union with [Spain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_spain "Kingdom of spain"), according to the [Constitution](/wiki/Colombian_Constitution_of_1991 "Colombian Constitution of 1991") of Cundinamarca, which was not recognized by the [Spanish Crown](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Spain "Monarchy of Spain"), which considered these territories to be part of the [Viceroyalty of New Granada](/wiki/Viceroyalty_of_New_Granada "Viceroyalty of New Granada").

## Republics in personal union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Personal_union&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Republics in personal union")]

Because heads of state and government of republics are ordinarily chosen from within the citizens of the state in question, sovereign republics very rarely share common leaders. A few examples are:

* Uniquely, the [President of France](/wiki/President_of_France "President of France") is *[ex officio](/wiki/Ex_officio "Ex officio")* a constitutional monarch (or, more accurately, [diarch](/wiki/Diarchy "Diarchy")) in neighboring [Andorra](/wiki/Andorra "Andorra"), with the title of [Co-Prince.](/wiki/Co-prince_of_Andorra "Co-prince of Andorra") This status was inherited from the role of the French monarchs in Andorra.
* From 1802 to 1804, the [First Consul](/wiki/First_Consul "First Consul") of the [French Republic](/wiki/First_French_Republic "First French Republic") and the President of the [Italian Republic](/wiki/Italian_Republic_(Napoleonic) "Italian Republic (Napoleonic)") was the same person: [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte"). Bonaparte continued as President of Italy even after he was proclaimed [Emperor of the French](/wiki/Emperor_of_the_French "Emperor of the French"), until he was proclaimed King of [Italy](/wiki/Napoleonic_Italy "Napoleonic Italy") in 1805.
* During the later stages of the [Spanish American Wars of Independence](/wiki/Spanish_American_Wars_of_Independence "Spanish American Wars of Independence"), [Simón Bolívar](/wiki/Sim%C3%B3n_Bol%C3%ADvar "Simón Bolívar") was simultaneously President of [Gran Colombia](/wiki/Gran_Colombia "Gran Colombia") (24 February 1819 - 4 May 1830), President of [Peru](/wiki/Peru "Peru") (10 February 1824 – 28 January 1827), and President of [Bolivia](/wiki/Bolivia "Bolivia") (12 August 1825 - 29 December 1825). Bolívar had, as President and military Commander-in-Chief of Colombia, led a Colombian army to secure Peruvian independence in 1824-25, and was given the office of President by the Patriot republican governments of both Peru and Bolivia (renamed in his honor from "Upper Peru") as an emergency measure to help secure independence from Spain. After the end of the war, Bolívar relinquished his Peruvian and Bolivian offices and returned to Colombia.
* In 1860 [Marthinus Wessel Pretorius](/wiki/Marthinus_Wessel_Pretorius "Marthinus Wessel Pretorius") was simultaneously elected as the president of [Transvaal](/wiki/South_African_Republic "South African Republic") and [Orange Free State](/wiki/Orange_Free_State "Orange Free State"). He tried to unify the two countries, but his efforts failed, leading to the [Transvaal Civil War](/wiki/Transvaal_Civil_War "Transvaal Civil War").

For example, [Joseph Clemens of Bavaria](/wiki/Joseph_Clemens_of_Bavaria "Joseph Clemens of Bavaria") (1671–1723) was [Prince-Bishop of Freising](/wiki/Prince-Bishop_of_Freising "Prince-Bishop of Freising") (1685–1694), [Prince-Bishop of Regensburg](/wiki/Prince-Bishop_of_Regensburg "Prince-Bishop of Regensburg") (1685–1694), [Prince-Elector of Cologne](/wiki/Prince-Elector_of_Cologne "Prince-Elector of Cologne") (1688–1723), [Prince-Bishop of Liège](/wiki/Prince-Bishop_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Prince-Bishop of Liège") (1694–1723) and [Prince-Bishop of Hildesheim](/wiki/Prince-Bishop_of_Hildesheim "Prince-Bishop of Hildesheim") (1702–1723).

3. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** 瀋陽王 ([Simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese "Simplified Chinese"): 沈阳王, [Pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): Shěnyáng Wáng; 심양왕; *Simyang Wang*).
